GoodTaste_Def: This is subjective. When we say that someone has good taste, it could mean that they have a deep understanding of all the aspects of a certain topic and that their personal bias is one that shows a true appreciation for said subject. It could also mean that they like things that others do, so as to follow a trend.
BadTaste_Def: Bad taste is noted when others do not approve of someone's choices or biases on a particular topic. It could mean that they are behind on following trends, or it could mean that they fail to see the depth or true value in the aforementioned topic and have shallow judgement.
GoodTaste_Example: This person has good taste because they have spent a lot of time consuming media from this particular topic and is able to identify strengths, weaknesses and areas of improvement for participants in the subject. They do not pass quick judgement and their opinions are held in esteem.
BadTaste_Example: This person has bad taste because they refuse to accept others' opinions on the subject. They remain ignorant to what may be a better option and feel as though they have the one true answer to what the best choice is.
Improve_Possible: People can improve their tastes by spending a lot of time in the subject matter. They can listen to expert opinions. They can absorb information from all angles before forming their own opinions and choices.

GoodTaste_Def: If someone has a good taste, then they like things that many people view are "good". It's a very subjective thing to have a "good taste", I think it depends on how you grew up. In certain areas, it'd be luxury things or elegant things that are defined as good taste. In other parts, it could just be like something that isn't as "Trendy" as what most people like. I'd also say that people with "good taste", tend to think on their own and don't follow what others like.
BadTaste_Def: I'd say someone with a "bad taste" doesn't really like things on their own, they tend to be a follower for what's popular so they don't end up with much of a taste at all. I'd also say it's when they like a lot of things that other people tend to dislike, so they'd have a "bad taste" compared. Again though, really depends on cultures and areas they grew up, what may be bad tastes where they are, could be good tastes elsewhere.
GoodTaste_Example: Myself, and as conceited as it sounds, I truly believe I have good taste compared to most people. I say myself because I really have a an amazingly broad spectrum of things I like compared to most people. Even if there's something I dislike, I can find a way to appreciate it, I break down everything into deeper parts and go over it, so I really know what my taste is and why it's that way. I can relate to everyone, while being so different from everyone, and I think "good taste" features a wide variety of unrelated things you'd like.
BadTaste_Example: One of my friends, he doesn't have any of his own tastes at all, just follows everyone else. I say it's bad because he doesn't have his own views on anything he likes, he just likes them because they're popular and follows the big trends with his friends or online. Not really much of a taste, so I view it as bad.
Improve_Possible: They can explore things they never have before, they can ask a wide variety of people to get more views from their tastes. If they can start appreciating things others like and seeing why they like it, they can imrpove their own taste in the same way, by appreciating things even if they dislike them.

It does not seek to bring attention to itself in a self-promoting way; it is quieter and more humble, more restrained, not ego-driven.
BadTaste_Def: Bad taste is what we think of as too attention-getting -- for example, loud, garish colors, or following shallow, popular fads, or acting in foolish, unconsidered ways, not having properly thought through how your actions will affect those around you, such as in rash actions or vulgar displays of emotion.
GoodTaste_Example: I have a neighbor who puts together her household in a beautiful way, not expensively, but with care as to how the furnishings and colors blend with each other.  She puts a lot of thought into the harmony of it.  She is also a caring person, who is kind and considerate with others, seems to know the right thing to say to bring them out and make their day brighter
BadTaste_Example: Well, that's easy, because there is another neighbor in this complex who knows little restraint and has lashed out with intemperate language,posting an abusive letter without her signature, blaming others for problems before finding out what the facts were. People like this do not take the time to take into consideration various factors -- but feel that everything is always only about them.
Improve_Possible: By education.  It is difficult for someone to judge a book, a painting, a room, without exposure and experience and education.  The more you see/read/hear/experience the better you understand how elements work together for beauty and harmony.  This is not to say there is only one kind of beauty, as it really is often in the eye of the beholder, but with education and experience you do learn to understand why YOU consider something beautiful.

BadTaste_Def: When someone has "bad taste" I tend to think they enjoy media, books, and food made for the lowest common denominator. They are easily entertained by things like reality television, Michael Bay films with tons of explosions, and their favorite music is whatever the latest generic popular rap/pop artist has on the radio.

He wears ill-fitting cargo shorts and immature graphic t-shirts with swear words on them or crude pictures. He drives a Toyota Corolla and spent more on speakers and tire rims than he paid for the actual car itself.
Improve_Possible: I think people can improve their "taste" by being exposed to different things that may make them see differently. Obviously, you can't force someone to "improve their taste" and trying to do so feels judgmental and pretentious. I think if an individual broadens their horizons to what they are willing to try, they may end up liking something with "good taste" more. What is considered "good taste" is subjective, but exposure to more complex and stimulating things is a good start.

GoodTaste_Def: Taste is about preference, therefore it is entirely subjective, even if the preference is based on objective qualities. I believe that nearly everyone believes they have "good taste," so when most people say "good taste," they mean that the taste in question is similar to their own.
BadTaste_Def: "Bad taste" would mean the opposite of "good taste." Say that I prefer a certain style of movie, possibly something like arthouse or experimental, and another person only likes faster-paced, mainstream theatrical releases. We would probably refer to each other's preferences as "bad taste," even though neither style necessarily has any more or less merit.
GoodTaste_Example: Right now I can only think of my son. Of all the people I can recall, he is the only one with a taste similar to mine. Since I believe I have "good taste" I raised my son to enjoy the same types of movies and books that I enjoy. Of course, I didn't force it upon him, and at first, he found many of the movies boring, but gradually, over time he began to appreciate the type of art I enjoy.
BadTaste_Example: In a rational sense, I don't believe in "bad taste" since everyone has personal preferences of what they can enjoy. However, oftentimes I still do judge others' taste in movies. One of my friends, in particular, can only watch action movies. They don't care about any of the other aspects of a movie, they only care about the action. If it doesn't have action they get bored. I consider their taste to be poor. It's a bad habit of mine.
Improve_Possible: I believe you can either improve your taste by constantly exposing yourself to the fine arts. If you see enough movies, visit enough museums, or read enough books, usually you will be able to develop a sense of what makes the art good to you. You will often times be able to distinguish the good art from the mediocre from the bad. It's still about preference though, and it is very hard to develop a taste in paintings and sculptures.

GoodTaste_Def: When I say that someone has good taste, I generally mean that I can see value and positive qualities in the art/music/literature that they like, even if I don't enjoy it myself.  However I must admit that much of the time, when I say that someone has good taste, it means that they share my taste, i.e. they admire the art/music/literature that I myself enjoy.
BadTaste_Def: When I say that someone has bad taste, I generally mean that I view negatively the art/music/literature that that person enjoys.  That is, I don't think it conveys any meaning, I don't think that the person who created it has any talent, and I don't see the value in it.  I have to admit that it often means that this is very subjective, in that it often means that I myself don't like the art/music/literature at all myself.
GoodTaste_Example: I have a friend who has excellent taste.  Her home is beautifully furnished, with many lovely pieces of furniture and decorative pieces that I admire.  Her clothes are really distinctive but not outlandish and I love the colors and styles she buys.  She is a musician and I always enjoy hearing the songs she plays on the piano for me.  She has recommended several books to me that I have really enjoyed.
BadTaste_Example: I have a family member who has very poor taste, in my opinion.  I listen to the music he plays for me, but I don't "get" them; the songs seem poorly executed and don't have anything to say.  He loves to decorate his apartment with pieces that look garish and outlandish to me, but he thinks that they are beautiful and coordinate well with each other.  The art on his walls doesn't appeal to me either.
Improve_Possible: They can learn about literature/art, etc., and become more analytical in their approach to it, appreciating more the artistry that goes into creating it.

GoodTaste_Def: To me good taste is defined by a certain reserve someone may have rather than a specific style. For example, someone who is vulgar or crass, to me, might have a different taste level than someone who holds themselves to a higher standard of behavior.
BadTaste_Def: Bad taste would be defined by a lack of manners or behavior not quite up to a certain innate standard that seems to be inborn with me. Being crude, crass or vulgar, for example, would be in low taste to me. I was born and raised in the southern United States , where historically there are behaviors that divide bad and good taste, regardless of any racial group or financial status. Some things just aren't done, or they would be considered tacky. It's weird because I don't remember ever being specifically told what was good and what was not, it was just understood, if that makes any sense.  It's hard to explain this and not feel like a snob.
GoodTaste_Example: Yes, I know several people who I think have great taste. My grandmothers come to mind, they were ladies to the T but also real women who exuded grace under extreme pressure, made it look so easy to manage home and hearth, raise several children successfully, I mean nobody wants to be the parent of a Jeffrey Dahmer right? The matriarchs of my family instilled in me the belief that I could be anything I wanted and how to be a lady while achieving my goals. I never heard a swear word from either of them, they didn't gossip, run the streets, stir up trouble or have their business aired around town. They were very much the type of women who were gracious, humble, grateful, intelligent, witty and just had this quality about them that is the essence of great taste.
BadTaste_Example: Well, I would have to say yes I know someone who I consider to have bad taste. There is a difference though between someone who doesn't know any better versus someone that knows how to act but ignores that fact. Again, to me taste and style are two different animals. Style can change and evolves, I think but taste is more of a character quality.  And I am a huge snob probably lol, although I would never tell anyone or show in any way that I thought someone acted in bad taste or had bad style either way. That would be in bad taste, in my opinion.
Improve_Impossible: To say whether or not they can improve their taste implies that something was wrong with their taste to begin with and I don't agree with that statement. Now that's not to say that person can't be educated about the actual techniques an artist might employ and from that knowledge have more insight about art or artists in general. But, I also believe that art is very personal and subjective. A person likes what they like. If they only like something because it's the popular opinion or someone told them to like it, well, then they are poseurs with no true substance, which is ironically ,in bad taste. l

GoodTaste_Def: Good taste' might an evaluation of a certain media in terms of an aesthetic appeal which acknowledges refinement, utility, and elegance with regards to an object of consumption.
BadTaste_Def: Bad taste' might be a pomposity, inelegance, and dirty extraneousness put upon an object of consumption.
GoodTaste_Example: The canonical example of 'good taste' is Steve Jobs, the conductor behind Apple's flow into mainstream culture with devices such as the iPod, iPhone, and iPad. The tastefulness lies in the refinement of the product, the willingness to sacrifice obvious utility with a discoverability hidden in simplicity. Elegance in refinement, a sacrifice to 'taste' which the market acknowledged as successful.
BadTaste_Example: The packaging of 'Monster' energy drinks has been done in bad taste: Lacking elegance, subtlety, and a sense of cohesion, the 'Monster' brand has instead relied on tactless, abrasive, and distasteful promotion for their product. It is almost ironic in its lack of tasteful promotion.
Improve_Possible: Exposure to more provocatively tasteful expressions... It needn't be extreme, though rather than dumping garbage upon the populace (such as YouTube, Netflix, Google search results and recommendations) to serve the algorithms, we might try to expose more tender and artful alternatives... I'm not sure by what other means society has to sway exposure. Asking people to read the NYTimes book reviews or the Guardian's culture reviews is a preposterous proposition, however the ways in which culture is being disseminated via the algorithms is need of a serious overhaul.
